title: Coming of Age in Second Life: An Anthropologist Explores the Virtually Human ‐ by Tom Boellstorff
abstract: Coming of Age in Second Life: An Anthropologist Explores the Virtually Human . Boellstorff, Tom . 2008 . Princeton, NJ : Princeton University Press . 328 pp. ISBN13: 978-0-691-13528-1 . $32.95 (cloth) . Virtual reality environments are proliferating on the Internet, with market researchers reporting dramatic growth in virtual worlds designed for children and adolescents (K-Zero, 2008). As one of the longest-lasting virtual worlds with 19 million registered subscribers, Second Life (SL) is in a unique position to demonstrate what occurs in these new intersections of technology and family life. But Second Life is not a conventional object for family scholarship. In Coming of Age in Second Life: An Anthropologist Explores the Virtually Human, Tom Boellstorff, professor of anthropology at the University of California, Irvine, records a brief period when this new culture was being formed. During the time of his research (2004–2007) Second Life was a relatively new and unknown society undergoing dramatic transformations. The product of human creativity and computer technology, it exists only online; residents and their self-designed physical environments are represented in three dimensions, displayed on computer screens using specialized software. Boellstorff has described the complex identities, relationships, and political and economic trends in this emerging social form, creating a lively and multilayered book that seems to include something for every reader. Although he states that he wrote the book for a broad audience because he believes that virtual worlds and cybersociality are growing in their importance and are not well understood, from my position as a family therapist I see other implications to his work beyond its focus on virtual realities. Using a social constructionist perspective, I think of relationships as cultures being created anew as members are added or new characteristics attach themselves to existing members: stepfamilies, for example, and families in adolescence. As a methodologically advanced study of a new social system in rapid transformation, this book has implications for all who study and work with couple and family relationships. Boellstorff's work challenges family scholars and practitioners to find ways of entering, participating in, and providing help for these small, fragile cultures that are continually being transformed by the intentional and unintentional choices of their residents. Second Life, the society studied in this book, is different in many ways from those studied by previous generations of anthropologists. It is a “virtual world” that depends on electronic media for its existence and therefore challenges the researcher to find appropriate methods (Jordan, 2009). It is not a game, even though it builds on game technologies, but instead has been compared with a neighborhood pub where people spend their free time, developing relationships as they engage in their favorite pastimes. Universities, corporations, and governments are also using Second Life for virtual classes, conferences, and festivals (see Jarmon, Lim, & Carpenter, 2009). Identity is a central phenomenon in people's experiences of SL. The citizens of virtual worlds only exist in their online manifestations—residents of Second Life are three-dimensional avatars, designed and “inhabited” by people in “real life” but often quite different from their authors. The real-life persons who have created these avatars have lives that may be similar to, or quite different from, the ones they live in the virtual world. A shy 50-year-old man whose body does not conform to media-promoted standards can create an identity as a tall, thin, assertive 20-year-old—and have a second identity as a seductive mature woman. It is not uncommon for a single person in real life to appear in multiple identities in SL, and some even adopt identities that are not human. Not only the residents of this society but also their physical environments exist solely in virtual space (Boellstorff offers a few illustrations). SL residents are able to own “land” where they create dramatic landscapes and buildings, yachts, swimming pools, and airplanes, with few economic limitations to their creativity. In these surroundings they spend time together conversing and sharing lectures, art collections, video, music, and sports events. Businesses flourish, selling objects (e.g., clothing), entertainment, and services (e.g., interior design) in the closed economy of SL. People meet, fall in love, get married, and go through divorces. And some residents engage in antisocial behavior, creating pressure for a legal system. Even in virtual space, conflicts arise when neighbors erect tall buildings that block a view or they open a club that plays loud music. Sexuality is controlled but ever present, and violence has been a problem from time to time. In Second Life, the newcomer is immediately challenged to create an identity and to learn basic skills. The first task involves selecting a screen name: a decision Boellstorff describes in detail. One must declare a sex, and an appearance—skin color, body type, and so forth—must be assembled from menus. Newcomers face many technological challenges, including the skills of moving in three dimensions on a two-dimensional screen, flying and teleporting to visit different regions. “Newbies”—often recognizable by their drab, generic appearance and their clumsiness—may be ignored by some more experienced residents but often receive helpful suggestions from those they meet in parks, clubs, and stores. Those who persist and develop essential skills generally move into developing relationships. The life span described in this book is brief; the long-term residents may have been in SL for only 3 years. In that time, however, significant relationships are formed, and residents respond strongly to changes in their social networks (including real-life deaths, which may not be discovered for some time after they occur). In extended quotations, Boellstorff's informants describe their decisions to join and remain in SL, including negotiating tensions between their online relationships and real-life marriages or jobs. They talk about their satisfactions and frustrations as well as unique encounters with SL traditions related to multiple time zones, connection problems, and computer crashes. One of the most important elements of SL, its “creationist capitalist” economy, seems to find nearly everyone involved in producing and selling goods and services in exchange for Linden Dollars, which are traded in the real world for other currencies. Given that the frequent changes in SL's technology disrupted some economic strategies, in some cases residents suffered serious consequences. Political activity, then, was another theme that appeared in many stories. Boellstorff's theoretical orientation and methods are especially well suited for the challenge of examining this new culture. Theoretically, Boellstorff sees serious limitations in the dominant view of culture as episteme—knowledge about reality. He prefers to see culture as techne—knowledge about how people perform the essential routines that create their reality. In Coming of Age, Boellstorff documents his initiation into the SL world and shares his own and his informants' perspectives on the joys and challenges of being part of a large-scale social experiment. He locates himself in the tradition of Franz Boaz, whose approach to anthropology he characterizes as immersing oneself in a culture, not expecting residents to describe the “rules” of their culture but instead working to gain an insider perspective from which one can gradually abstract relevant themes. Therefore he chose to study SL as a participant observer. Boellstorff entered SL much as if he were landing on an island, where he initially would depend on a small number of informants who would help him learn to function in their social world. He was clear about his identity as an anthropologist (using the online name Tom Bukowski) as well as his mission, spreading the word that he wanted opportunities to learn about all aspects of the society. Over his time in SL he conducted formal interviews in individual and group formats but he also shopped in local stores, developed a wide variety of personal relationships, visited residents in their homes, and became a valued member of community organizations. He had access to computer-specific tools, including the ability to design his appearance and shape his own living space so that they would help to clarify his identity, and he was able to gather large volumes of text conversation. This approach was ideal for study of a culture that was changing so fast—and was so complex—that its members themselves could not describe the culture of which they were a part. SL is not the first virtual society to be noticed by scholars. The implications of computer-mediated relationships have been explored for decades (e.g., Turkle, 1984). Computer games, in the 1970s and 1980s, developed into online “multiuser dungeons,” where players could pool their resources to create complex environments. Even before the term “virtual” gained its conventional meanings related to the Internet, groups of people established unique sets of relational patterns and cultural meanings meeting in local computer networks. More recently, social networking sites are producing new kinds of virtual relationships. Boellstorff, who uses the term “virtual” in unique ways, suggests that cyber-technology is merely a new version of an inherently human history. For example, he observes that newspapers in earlier generations created communities by connecting physically separated populations in shared networks of meaning. Viewed in this way, SL is not inherently different from the family groupings, cities, religious institutions, political parties, sports teams, universities, and other cultures that have been more typically the focus of academic attention. He claims, “It is in being virtual that we are human” (p. 29). Even if it is not unique in its essential symbolic nature, however, SL seems to offer family scholars a unique opportunity to examine many levels of symbolic exchange interacting in an emerging society. SL residents, in the period of time chronicled by Boellstorff, communicated to a great extent through text, generating a record that could be preserved and examined (he refers to collecting 10,000 pages of transcripts). Users were aware of being cocreators of their unique environment, and they discussed it at length in private as well as public conversations. They critiqued their own virtual world and both celebrated and challenged the ways in which it influenced their identities and relationships. The environment was also being studied and manipulated by the corporate management of Linden Labs, which operates SL as a profit-generating business. Therefore all residents were not equal; mixed into the population were engineers, marketing specialists, and security personnel attempting to limit the negative aspects of SL culture and maximize its appeal to newcomers. Their communication with the rest of the population is part of the record. Coming of Age in Second Life is often mentioned in connection with Donna Haraway's Cyborg Manifesto (1985/1991). Haraway, writing in a time before SL could have been predicted, described her fantasy cyborgs—blurring the boundaries between human and machine—as ironic images that could inspire women to move past binary thinking and imagine their potential free from biological constraints. Neither one thing nor the other, cyborgs could be infinitely varied, embodying the biological and technological elements that best fit the needs of the moment. In recent years this potential is being actualized (Gane, 2006). With help from technology, people are able to augment or replace parts of themselves that do not suit their purposes. Boellstorff finds SL residents saying that their avatars embody some of their real-life characteristics, but they feel more truly themselves when online. He finds many people choosing to change their age, race, gender, or a combination of these, but he also finds individuals with real-world disabilities who speak of their SL experience as an opportunity to live without barriers. Haraway is often cited by those who have speculated, over the past 20 years, on the emergence of a “posthuman” society. Hayles (2006) suggests, for example, that electronic media have transformed life so that the individual person is losing significance when compared with “distributed cultural cognitions.” This portrayal has both optimistic and pessimistic potentials; Hayles expresses hope that relational and distributed agency may improve the species by overcoming the effects of uncontrolled free will. Boellstorff, however, expresses doubts about the term “posthuman” and its portrayal of humans and technology as merging. Technology, he says, is not a recent arrival but is, in fact, the central feature of humanity, both a tool and a product of human effort. The printing press changed human existence, but we remain human, and new forms of techne bring out new aspects of human potential. Tom Boellstorff has created a thoughtful and engaging, thorough picture of SL and its residents. In the process, he continually steps back to question the contributions of scholarship in the larger project of human existence. This award-winning book is rapidly becoming a cornerstone of a growing body of virtual reality literature and, if current trends continue, Coming of Age in Second Life is likely to earn a place on required reading lists for human development and family studies courses.
